data.title: The stock market has trigged its first ‘proper buy signal’ since Liberation Day – HSBC’s Kettner
data.description:
Max Kettner, chief multi-asset strategist at HSBC, believes the stock market (SP500), (COMP:IND), (DJI) has triggered its first proper buy signal since the Liberation Day episode, and he expects Monday’s closing low of $63.43 to hold as the near-term bottom.

The strategist sees bullish conditions emerging across the broader risk asset spectrum, including equities and credit spreads, and echoed sentiments by Yardeni Research’s Ed Yardeni, who said Monday was the market’s bottom and maintained his year-end S&P 500 (SP500) target of 7,700 despite recent volatility.

Speaking in an interview with CNBC, Kettner explained that while high-frequency data on labor markets and consumer spending remained solid throughout March, he had been wary due to market positioning. That changed last week when both systematic and discretionary positioning shifted dramatically.

“As of last week, both systematic positioning, but particularly discretionary positioning—particularly around the hedging side of things—has now really triggered the first proper buyer signal for the broader risk asset spectrum really since the liberation day episode,” Kettner said.

The bullish outlook is supported by multiple technical indicators and sentiment surveys. Kettner pointed to momentum signals, put-call ratios, skews, and investor sentiment surveys including AAII and Investor Intelligence as confirming the shift. He emphasized that investors are now heavily over-hedged across the board, a condition that historically supports a market bottom.

Despite the optimism, Kettner warned that the upcoming core CPI reading remains a critical factor for market stability. He suggested that a high reading of 0.4% or 0.5% would be “not great news” for the recent momentum.

HSBC utilizes what Kettner calls a “danger zone” model, identifying the 4.5% level on the 10-year Treasury yield (US10Y) as a threshold where risk assets begin to struggle.

The market is currently only about 15 basis points away from this level, leaving what Kettner described as an uncomfortable margin for safety.

If a hot inflation print pushes yields into that danger zone, Kettner warned that virtually nothing would offer protection.

“Nothing works, whether it’s EM, whether it’s credit, whether it’s rates, whether it’s equities,” he explained, adding that such a scenario “will spell trouble indeed really for the entire asset class spectrum except for the greenback.”

data.title: Dollar eases as geopolitical uncertainty keeps markets cautious: Currency Recap
data.description:
The U.S. dollar was down on Monday, as investors assessed shifting geopolitical developments and cautious risk sentiment tied to the ongoing U.S.-Iran conflict.

The dollar index (DXY), which measures the greenback against a basket of major currencies, was last down 0.07% to $99.96 but remains up about 1.72% for the year to date.

Dollar weekly moves and key drivers:

Over the past week, the index slipped 0.29%, with the U.S. dollar largely supported earlier in the period by safe-haven demand as tensions in the Middle East escalated.

 

Concerns over a prolonged conflict, including threats of intensified strikes and continued disruption to the Strait of Hormuz, drove oil prices sharply higher, raising inflation risks and reinforcing demand for the dollar as a safe-haven asset.

The greenback also drew support as rising energy prices  and geopolitical uncertainty led markets to reassess the global interest rate outlook, with expectations for policy easing pushed further out.

However, sentiment shifted intermittently during the week, with the dollar weakening at times on signs of potential de-escalation, including ceasefire discussions and mixed messaging from U.S. officials, which helped ease risk aversion and supported risk-sensitive assets.

The resulting back-and-forth in geopolitical headlines kept currency markets volatile, with traders reacting to each shift in the outlook for the conflict while remaining cautious amid uncertainty over its duration and economic impact.

During the period, global markets were influenced by developments in the U.S.-Iran conflict, sharp swings in oil prices, evolving inflation expectations, and broader shifts in risk sentiment.

U.S. Treasury yields were mixed overall, with the 10-year Treasury yield (US10Y) holding steady at 4.33% over the week, while the 2-year yield (US2Y) rose 4 basis points to 3.86%.

Currency Highlights:

Major currency movements (March 30 to April 06)

 

Euro (EUR:USD)

+0.43%

Pound Sterling (GBP:USD)

+0.05%

Japanese Yen (JPY:USD)

-0.16%

Chinese Yuan (CNY:USD)

+0.36%

Swiss Franc (CHF:USD)

-0.28%

Australian Dollar (AUD:USD)

+0.79%

Canadian Dollar (CAD:USD)

-0.09%
 

The Japanese Yen (JPY:USD) was last down 0.09%, with the currency easing as risk sentiment showed signs of stabilization despite ongoing geopolitical tensions.

During the week, the yen saw choppy moves, strengthening on heightened U.S.-Iran conflict risks and safe-haven demand, while pulling back on intermittent optimism around potential de-escalation.

The Chinese Yuan (CNY:USD) was last up 0.14%, with the currency supported as regional sentiment steadied despite ongoing geopolitical uncertainty.

During the week, the yuan tracked mixed developments in Asian markets and economic data, including signs of moderating manufacturing momentum and steady services activity, while broader risk sentiment remained tied to U.S.-Iran conflict headlines.

The Euro (EUR:USD) was last up 0.24%, with the currency holding gains as regional sentiment remained mixed amid ongoing geopolitical uncertainty. 

Over the week, the euro tracked developments in European markets and economic data, including rising inflation, higher unemployment, and fluctuating risk sentiment tied to U.S.-Iran conflict headlines.

data.title: The Arena Group teams up with Playwire to boost ad monetization
data.description:
• The Arena Group (AREN) announced a partnership with Playwire, a private company, to enhance its ad monetization capabilities and to scale its premium digital ad space.
  • Under this deal, Playwire’s direct sales team will sell Flex Suite ad units across Arena Group’s (AREN) entire portfolio of brands, boosting advertising reach and efficiency.
  • The stock is trading at $1.98, up 1.54% during market hours as of Apr. 6.
  • Source: Press Release

data.title: Shell, TotalEnergies among companies eyeing U.S. Gulf field stake - Reuters
data.description:
European energy majors Shell (SHEL) and TotalEnergies (TTE) are among companies that may be interested in taking a majority stake in one of the U.S. Gulf's most promising sites, Reuters reported late Thursday, as interest in North American energy prospects ‌rises due to the Middle East war.

BP (BP) and Spain's Repsol (REPYF) (REPYY) also may be interested, and Chevron (CVX) is expected to consider a bid, according to the report.

Two of the owners of the Shenandoah offshore field - Blackstone-backed Beacon Offshore Energy, the operator of the field, and ​HEQ Deepwater, which is owned by Quantum Capital Group and Houston Energy - launched a sale process for their ​stakes in recent days, offering potential buyers 51% of the project, the report said.

Not all the parties may end up bidding, and the deal valuation will depend on ​factors including how much of Shenandoah is ultimately sold and what happens with oil prices, according to the report.

Shenandoah is an ultra deepwater field that is seen as having some of the greatest potential in the U.S. Gulf ​region, industry experts say.

data.title: LyondellBasell downgraded by BofA as rally seen as overdone
data.description:
Shares of LyondellBasell Industries (LYB) face a more cautious outlook after analysts at Bank of America Securities downgraded the chemical producer to Underperform from Neutral, arguing that much of the recent upside driven by geopolitical disruptions is already reflected in the stock.

The report, dated April 6, comes after a sharp rally in chemical equities tied to higher energy prices and supply dislocations stemming from the Iran conflict. While those dynamics have boosted margins and near-term earnings expectations, BofA analysts led by Matthew DeYoe warn investors may be extrapolating temporary conditions too far into the future.

Conflict tailwinds seen as fleeting

BofA described LyondellBasell (LYB) as one of the “largest beneficiaries” of the current environment, citing its exposure to petrochemical pricing and its leverage to U.S. feedstock advantages. However, the bank emphasized that these benefits are likely cyclical rather than structural.

“Shares have rallied on Iran conflict tailwinds which we expect to be fleeting,” the analysts wrote, noting that pricing strength tied to supply disruptions could fade as markets normalize.

Even under a higher-for-longer oil price scenario, the firm expects petrochemical pricing to peak in the near term before weakening, particularly as capacity additions and normalization pressures reassert themselves.

Higher estimates, but lower conviction

Despite the downgrade, BofA raised its earnings forecasts for the company. The firm increased its 2026-28 projections to reflect stronger product margins and elevated energy prices, with 2026 earnings before interest, taxes, depreciation and amortization expected to climb meaningfully.

The bank also lifted its price objective to $68 from $55, reflecting improved near-term earnings power. Still, that target implies limited upside from current levels, reinforcing the more cautious rating.

Market pricing in peak conditions

A key concern highlighted in the report is valuation. BofA argues that current stock prices already discount “peak” or near-peak earnings conditions, leaving little room for further multiple expansion.

The analysis suggests investors are effectively pricing in several quarters of elevated profitability, even as the underlying conditions supporting those profits remain inherently unstable.

Risk of oversupply returning

Looking beyond the current cycle, BofA sees a re-emergence of oversupply risks across petrochemical markets, particularly if capacity closures fail to materialize.

The report notes that global utilization rates remain relatively low and that significant capacity additions, especially in polyethylene, could weigh on margins in coming years. Without meaningful production cuts, the market could revert to a structurally oversupplied state.

Balancing short-term strength with long-term pressures

While LyondellBasell (LYB) continues to benefit from strong cash flows and improved margins in the current environment, BofA’s downgrade underscores a broader theme across the chemicals sector: a disconnect between short-term earnings strength and longer-term fundamentals.

The bank concludes that while near-term conditions remain supportive, investors should be cautious about assuming durability in those trends, particularly as geopolitical effects fade and supply-demand balances normalize.

BofA also downgraded Dow (DOW) following its recent rally.

data.title: BP shareholders should reject revised climate disclosure, ISS says
data.description:
Proxy advisor Institutional Shareholder Services has ​recommended shareholders vote against a plan by BP's (BP) board to drop two older climate reporting commitments ahead of its April 23 annual meeting, Reuters reported Saturday.

BP's (BP) board has called ​for a vote to retire two resolutions from 2015 and 2019 requiring company-specific ⁠climate reporting, arguing that newer, standardized frameworks are clearer and easier to compare across companies, a move ISS called "unprecedented in the U.K. context."

"We do not consider ​the board's argument that the prior resolutions detract from the clarity of reporting and standardized disclosures to constitute a sufficiently compelling case to offset the ​concerns for 'retiring' the relevant disclosures," ISS said.

The proxy advisor also said investors should vote against a measure that would allow BP (BP) to hold online-only shareholder meetings.

The ISS recommendations follow a broadening climate campaign against BP (BP) by some European investors, ​representing less than 0.3% of the company's owners, led by Dutch activist shareholder group Follow This.

BP (BP) also named Carol Howle, who served as interim CEO before Meg O'Neill started the top job last week, as deputy CEO, saying she would oversee the ‌company's ongoing portfolio review and strategy development.

data.title: AMC shares jump on record Easter weekend led by 'Super Mario'
data.description:
AMC Entertainment Holdings (AMC) surged over 15% on Monday after the theater chain saw a jump in ticket sales during the Easter weekend, all thanks to the blockbuster debut from The Super Mario Galaxy Movie.

Shares of the Leawood, Kansas-based company were trading at $1.30.

A sequel to the Nintendo-inspired franchise, the movie reportedly generated a global box office debut of over $370M, continuing the success of its 2023 predecessor. Directed by Aaron Horvath and Michael Jelenic and featuring voice actors Chris Pratt, Jack Black, Anya Taylor-Joy, and Charlie Day, The Super Mario Galaxy Movie follows Mario and Luigi’s adventure into outer space to face off against Bowser and his son, Bowser Jr.

AMC said more than 6M people attended AMC theatres in the U.S. or the Odeon Cinemas internationally from April 1 through April 5, leading to the company achieving the highest combined global admissions and food & beverage revenue over the holiday weekend in AMC’s 106-year history.

The theatre chain also benefitted from Super Mario-themed merchandise, including collectibles such as the Yoshi popcorn bucket and Luma LED popcorn bucket, it said.

“We have long said that theatrical exhibition is strongest when there is a compelling and diverse slate of films for guests to enjoy. This was an extraordinarily successful holiday frame for AMC and ODEON and yet another example of that dynamic in action,” said AMC CEO Adam Aron.

Other than the Super Mario movie, AMC benefited from a solid lineup of new releases, including The Drama, starring Zendaya and Robert Pattinson, as well as from the sustained momentum from the sci-fi film Project Hail Mary and Disney’s animated movie Hoppers.

AMC stock closed over 8% higher on Thursday. Overall, the stock has lost over 20% so far this year, compared to the nearly 4% fall in the broader S&P 500 Index.

data.title: Sumitomo Mitsui Financial & Nippon Life eye ¥500B private credit fund
data.description:
• On April 6, a major move is being discussed where Sumitomo Mitsui Financial Group (SMFG) and Nippon Life Insurance plan to create a large private credit fund of ~¥500B ($3.1B), showing strong growth in Japan’s private lending market.
  • The fund will reportedly focus on leveraged buyouts, real estate, and mezzanine loans, which can offer investors higher yields than traditional bonds and stocks.
  • This major step, along with global firms like Apollo Global Management (APO), Blackstone (BX), and KKR & Co. (KKR) entering Japan, signals that financing options are expanding with increased competition, reported Bloomberg.

data.title: Wall Street trims gains as Iran rejects ceasefire proposal
data.description:
Wall Street shifted higher on Monday as traders remained focused on developments in the Middle East, including the status of the Strait of Hormuz.

The tech-focused Nasdaq Composite (COMP:IND) was last +0.3%. At the same time, the benchmark S&P 500 (SP500) was +0.2%, and the blue-chip Dow (DJI) was +0.3%. 

On a sector-by-sector basis, eight of the 11 S&P segments were in the green, with financials at the top. At the other end of the spectrum, materials has been the weakest performer. 

President Donald Trump has given Iran until 8 p.m. Eastern time Tuesday to reopen the Strait of Hormuz, warning that failure to comply could trigger U.S. attacks on key infrastructure across the country.

The U.S., Iran, and regional mediators are negotiating a potential 45-day ceasefire that could pave the way for ending the war, Axios reported. 

U.S. Treasury yields climbed across the curve. The 10-year Treasury yield (US10Y) rose 2 basis points to 4.32%, while the 2-year yield (US2Y) moved up 3 basis points to 3.84%. The 30-year yield (US30Y) edged higher by 1 basis point to 4.88%.

On the economic front, March U.S. ISM Services PMI Index data arrived at 54.0 versus the. 54.7 consensus and 56.1 in February figures.

As for stocks that were on the move, Seagate Technology (STX) climbed +5.9%, while shares of Datadog (DDOG) dropped -3.9%.

data.title: Warren, Wyden press UI vendors over alleged failures in unemployment systems
data.description:
U.S. Sens. Elizabeth Warren and Ron Wyden have sent a series of sharply worded letters to major government technology vendors, raising concerns that their systems may be disrupting unemployment benefits and demanding detailed answers about how those platforms operate.

The letters, dated April 5 and sent to executives at Deloitte, Thomson Reuters (TRI) and Geographic Solutions, argue that states’ growing reliance on private contractors to modernize unemployment insurance systems has created new risks for jobless Americans trying to access benefits.

Lawmakers question ‘black box’ systems

In the letters, the senators describe a system increasingly dependent on outside vendors, many of whom provide off-the-shelf software or AI-driven tools that states have limited ability to audit or customize.

They warn that these systems often function as opaque ‘black boxes,’ with vendors sometimes declining to disclose how eligibility or fraud determinations are made, citing proprietary technology.

That lack of transparency, the lawmakers argue, can have serious consequences when automated systems make mistakes, particularly when state officials defer to algorithmic decisions without fully reviewing them.

Automation, AI under scrutiny

A major focus of the inquiry is the growing use of artificial intelligence in unemployment systems, including tools that verify identity, flag fraud and assess eligibility.

The senators say such systems have produced errors ranging from incorrectly flagging legitimate claims as fraudulent to misinterpreting basic information in applications.

They also cite reports of glitches that delay or halt payments, as well as cases where minor discrepancies or shared addresses triggered fraud alerts, potentially cutting off benefits for eligible workers.

While automation can streamline administrative work, the lawmakers emphasize that federal law requires human oversight in eligibility determinations, warning that overreliance on AI could undermine that standard.

Past system failures highlighted

The letters point to a history of troubled technology rollouts.

For Deloitte, the senators reference prior state projects that experienced technical failures, including system outages and processing errors that delayed payments for large numbers of claimants.

In the case of Thomson Reuters (TRI), the inquiry highlights concerns around fraud-detection software developed by its subsidiary Pondera Solutions, which has faced complaints alleging it led to wrongful denials or reductions of public benefits.

For Geographic Solutions, lawmakers cite a 2022 cyber incident that disrupted unemployment and job-search systems across multiple states, as well as earlier implementation issues that created administrative backlogs.

Broad request for data and accountability

The senators are seeking extensive information about how these companies design, test and maintain their systems, including what data their algorithms rely on, how errors are identified and corrected, and whether states have visibility into or control over the technology.

They also asked for details on contract values, performance outcomes and whether modernization efforts have improved payment accuracy and timeliness.

Responses are due by April 23.

Stakes for workers

At the center of the inquiry is the role unemployment benefits play as a financial safety net. The senators argue that when systems fail, whether due to coding errors, flawed data or opaque algorithms, the consequences can be immediate and severe, affecting recipients’ ability to pay for housing, food and healthcare.

The letters signal growing scrutiny in Washington over the intersection of public benefits and private-sector technology, particularly as states continue to modernize systems that millions of Americans rely on during periods of joblessness.

Seeking Alpha is asking for comment from the vendors.
